## A primer on short read assembly
<center>
![](/images/dna_laptop.png){style="width:150px; background: white; border-radius:5px; border: white solid 5px"}
</center>

Illumina reads are too short and numerous to use traditional overlap-layout-consensus assemblers as such an approach would be far too computationally intensive. Instead, we use De Bruijn graph based assemblers. Briefly, these operate as follows:

1.  All reads are broken down into k-length overlapping fragments (<u id='kmer'>k-mers</u>). e.g. if we choose a <u id='kmer'>k-mer</u> size of 5 bp, the following two sequences (blue) would be broken down into the <u id='kmer'>k-mers</u> below them (red):

<center>
![](/images/reads_aligned.png){style="border-radius: 10px; width: 400px"}
</center>

2.  All <u id='kmer'>k-mers</u> are linked to other <u id='kmer'>k-mers</u> which match with a k-1 length overlap (i.e. that overlap by all but one base):

<center>
![](/images/graph_simple.png){style="border-radius: 10px; width: 800px"}
</center>

3.  Paths are routed through the graph and longer contigs are generated:

<center>
![](/images/graph_complex.png){style="border-radius: 10px; width: 800px"}
</center>

The example here is a vast oversimplification of the complexity of a De Bruijn graph (i.e. there are no branches!). Routing through the graph is never as simple as this as some <u id='kmer'>k-mers</u> will lead to multiple <u id='kmer'>k-mers</u>, which can result in the break point of a contig. This is especially true for complex metagenomic data.

Generally speaking, the shorter the <u id='kmer'>k-mer</u>, the more branches there will be, the trickier the graph is to resolve, so the resulting contigs are smaller. Assemblers usually perform better with longer <u id='kmer'>k-mer</u> lengths but even then there might not be enough depth of sequencing to generate all k-mers that form overlaps, therefore leading to break points. Finding the right <u id='kmer'>k-mer</u> size usually involves testing several.

Fortunately, the assembler we will use, `MEGAHIT`, allows us to build an assembly using multiple <u id='kmer'>k-mer</u> lengths iteratively. The other great advantage about `MEGAHIT` is that it is quick and efficient. We will use `MEGAHIT` on our data soon, but first there is an additional processing step for our sequences...

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#Tippy tooltips
#Note, &#013; is a newline entitiy (\n does not work)
tippy::tippy_this(elementId = "kmer", 
                  tooltip = "Substrings of length k within a DNA/RNA sequence. 
                              The 1-mers of 'AGCT' are 'A', 'G', 'C', and 'T'. 
                              Its 2-mers are 'AG', 'GC' and 'CT'. 
                              Its 3-mers are 'AGC' and GCT'. 
                              Its 4-mer is 'AGCT'.",
                  arrow = TRUE, placement = "bottom")
```
